title: Welcome
text:
This page hosts all slides for presentations given during SPD’s Brinner and Learn series.
Each chapter title to the left is a link to a slide deck.

These slides are being developed by the SPD team.
Each deck will open in its own tab.
You may want to type “s” at the start of each deck to open the speaker notes.
Some of these slides may end up being adopted as a more formal SPD practice.

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
20 second make refresh
output1: input1 input2
  command

input2: sourceA sourceB
  write_input2.sh

output is tracked against its dependencies output for one step can be a dependency for another

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Agenda

Build Systems
Data Pipelines
Implications for our work
Discussion


There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.
– Phil Karlton

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Build system
A build system is a tool (or library/package) for:

Declaring build “targets” (outputs)
Defining the steps required to create those targets
Executing those steps

it usually also includes a mechanism for caching:

Identifying what the inputs are to a step (i.e. code files)
Determining if the outputs are stale (outdated)
Determining what steps need to be re-run based on staleness

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Data Pipelines
A data pipeline system is a tool (or library/package) for:

Declaring build “targets” (outputs)
Defining the steps required to create those targets
Executing those steps

it usually also includes a mechanism for caching:

Identifying what the inputs are to a step (i.e. data, code files)
Determining if the outputs are stale (outdated)
Determining what steps need to be re-run based on staleness

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Task runner
A task runner is a tool that runs tasks without caching. They may or may not support dependency resolution.
Examples of task runners:

just
npm run
Shell scripts

A build system that ignores its caches can be used as a task runner

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Same idea, different names



Build systems
Data pipelines




Target / artifact
Table / dataset / object


Rule
Script / transform / job


Dependency
Upstream input


Incremental rebuild
Incremental refresh / backfill


Cache
Materialization / snapshot


Hermetic build
Reproducible run (pinned code + inputs)


Test
Data quality check / invariant

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Same graph, different choices



Invalidation

Timestamps (make)
Content hashes (Docker)
Freshness windows (data pipelines)

Scheduling & execution

Local
CI runners
Distributed schedulers + compute engines




Determinism / hermeticity

Pinned toolchains + inputs
External systems
Mutable/Immutable data



determinism: Effectively, what is a declared dependency, vs. what is part of the environment
Don’t linger too long on this slide


What does “success” mean?

Build passes tests
Data passes invariants, freshness, and anomaly checks

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Enabling latest
If you treat data and code both as dependencies, you can very easily start to talk about the latest version of a data object (or dataset) in the same way you can talk about the latest version of an app or package

frequently (for us), code is what changes.

How often does relevant data update vs. codebase? Yearly? Weekly? Immediate?

This does not need to be a literal latest tag


Drop a link to working build in Teams/Slack/etc.

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Enabling versions/permalinks
If inputs and outputs (possibly caches) can be accessed independently, this can also enable versioning effectively

Code inputs (git checkout v1.2.3)
Data inputs (/datasets/my-dataset/2026-01-01.csv)
Built outputs

permalink
ETag
report_2026-01-01.pdf
index-Cwp2LJ1J.js

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Continuous Publication

If everything is a build, then everything can be a deploy
If it can be deployed, it could be deployed to PROD

The CI/CD pipeline is our Continuous Publication mechanism. It is possible to track latest and have up-to-date information, while still checking against tests, model expectations, invariants, and data quality checks.
It is also possible to mark old versions as inaccurate or deprecated

⚠️ The information or analysis in this report are no longer sound. See an updated version at foo.rmi.org/1234


This idea can sound really scary to people. It’s important to have faith in your automated checks. It’s also possible to have human-in-the-loop approval steps

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Caching and archiving

Identifying what to cache/store

What is expensive to calculate? (Cache)
What can we not re-create? (Archive)
Do we have concerns about an upstream dependency changing history/going offline?



What is cached often determines where Web page –> CDN batch job dataset –> Internal blob

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Tech Stack

How you want to handle dependencies determines what is the right tool
What tool you’re using determines how you handle dependencies

These affect system design (what is in or out of a system boundary), sometimes invisibly

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
A closer look at make
Make is a universal glue, and as such is worth examining as both a toy model and a well- and commonly-used tool.

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Language-specific pipelines
Using lanugage-speficic pipelines (targets) gives an easy way to track both code objects and data as “inputs”, and add them to the dependency graph.
It also gives them better syntax (familiar to devs and often just more expressive/easier to use than make)

automatic dependency tracking of more of the environment

title: Build Systems and Data Pipelines
text:
Baby’s first data pipeline
https://github.com/IndianaCHE/Detailed-SSP-Reports/blob/master/run.R

title: Test-Driven Development [TDD]
text:
Step 1: Write a Test (Red Phase)

Identify a small, testable unit of functionality.
Write a test that asserts the expected behavior.
Run the test (it should fail since the implementation doesn’t exist yet).


import pytest

def test_addition():
    assert add(2, 3) == 5  # Function doesn't exist yet

title: Test-Driven Development [TDD]
section: Step 2: Write Minimal Code to Pass the Test (Green Phase)
text:
Step 2: Write Minimal Code to Pass the Test (Green Phase)

Implement only the necessary code to make the test pass.


def add(a, b):
    return a + b


Run the test again (it should pass now).

title: Test-Driven Development [TDD]
text:
Step 3: Refactor the Code (Refactor Phase)

Clean up the implementation without changing behavior.
Ensure all tests still pass after refactoring.


def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    return a + b  # Adding types

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
section: Why Are Some Repos Hard to Use?
text:
Why Are Some Repos Hard to Use?

The path from “repo” to “execution” is usually unclear
“What am I supposed to run?”
“Which files are”active”, and which are historical debris?”
“Do I change code, config, or data to do the next run?”


Purpose: start from familiar pain instead of definitions.
Suggested talk track: “Most confusing repos do contain code. The problem is usually not missing code. The problem is that the path from repo to actual execution is unclear.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
The Core Problem

Reusable logic is mixed with one-off glue
Execution depends on tribal knowledge
Inputs are implicit and not declared
Reproducing a run becomes a scavenger hunt


Purpose: establish the central idea in one sentence.
Suggested talk track: “A repo can be full of files and still be operationally vague. If a new teammate cannot tell what to run and what controls the run, the software is not clear enough yet.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
section: One Example Repo, Three Different Kinds of Thing
text:
One Example Repo, Three Different Kinds of Thing
repo/
  src/project/model.py
  scripts/run_model.py
  model-run --config configs/base.yaml

src/project/model.py is library code: reusable logic
scripts/run_model.py is a script: often one runnable task
model-run ... is the application interface (in this case a CLI)


Purpose: replace fuzzy analogy with one concrete example.
Suggested talk track: “I want one picture in your head. Same repo, different jobs. The module in src/ holds reusable logic, the script runs one workflow, and the CLI is the supported doorway other people should use.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
What Is a Library?

Code that is meant to be imported and reused
Often: Functions, classes, and modules with precise behavior
Should never actually “do anything” just because it is imported
Unit tests should focus here, this is where stable behavior lives

def run_model(records, factors, threshold):
    ...

Purpose: define library in practical terms.
Suggested talk track: “If I import your module to use one function, it should not suddenly pull data, write files, or parse command-line arguments. Libraries are building blocks.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
What Is a Script?

A “executable” wrapper around one task
Often: Parses inputs, calls library code, writes outputs
Should stay thin so the logic is still reusable

def main():
    config = load_config(...)
    records = load_inputs(config)
    results = run_model(records, ...)
    write_outputs(results, config.output_dir)

Purpose: explain script as the missing middle layer.
Suggested talk track: “Scripts are often the first honest runnable thing in a project. They are not bad. They become a problem only when they silently become the whole architecture.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
What Is an Application?

A supported way for a person or system to use the workflow
Often: a CLI, API, scheduled job, pipeline step, or web application
An application still needs an entry point
The entry point is how that application actually gets invoked


Purpose: define application by user experience, not by prestige.
Suggested talk track: “An application does not have to be fancy. A clear CLI can absolutely be an application if it is the intended, supported way to run the workflow.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
What Actually Runs?

python -m package.module
a main() function
a CLI command like model-run --config configs/base.yaml
an API endpoint or scheduled job
a notebook cell, which works, but usually weakly


Purpose: translate entry points into user questions.
Suggested talk track: “Entry point is just the technical name for where execution starts. For most users, the important version is simpler: what do I run, and what controls the run?”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
The Common “Modeling” Pipeline

flowchart LR
    A[Entry point] --> B[Load config]
    B --> C[Load input data]
    C --> D[Call model library]
    D --> E[Write outputs]




flowchart LR
    A[Entry point] --> B[Load config]
    B --> C[Load input data]
    C --> D[Call model library]
    D --> E[Write outputs]







The library only provides logic
The entry point actually assembles the run
The config and input data determine the specific execution


Purpose: show the whole run path in one picture.
Suggested talk track: “When people say ‘the model runs,’ they usually mean this whole chain runs. The library is one important piece, but it is not the whole runnable system.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
section: Model Code, Configuration, Data, and Outputs Are Distinct
text:
Model Code, Configuration, Data, and Outputs Are Distinct

flowchart TB
    code[Code<br/>Reusable logic]
    config[Config<br/>Run instructions]
    data[Input data<br/>Domain facts]
    output[Outputs<br/>Results of the run]

    code --> run((Run))
    config --> run
    data --> run
    run --> output




flowchart TB
    code[Code<br/>Reusable logic]
    config[Config<br/>Run instructions]
    data[Input data<br/>Domain facts]
    output[Outputs<br/>Results of the run]

    code --> run((Run))
    config --> run
    data --> run
    run --> output







Code dictates how the software behaves
Configuration specifies how the run should execute
Data provides the domain facts for that run
Outputs are what the run produced


Purpose: establish the four-part model cleanly.
Suggested talk track: “A lot of repo confusion is category confusion. We blur code, config, data, and outputs together, then wonder why the project is hard to reason about.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
Two “Input” Artifacts, Different Intuition


Config file
release_year: 2026
portfolio_path: s3://team-data/portfolios/q1.parquet
threshold: 0.85
output_dir: outputs/2026-q1

Model run configuration
I/O data paths
etc.
Mostly instructions? Probably config.


Input data file
account_id, balance, sector, region
1042, 1500000, cement, NA
1043, 230000, steel, EU

Domain input, facts or records
The “thing” being modeled
Mostly records? Probably data.



Purpose: make config vs data obvious and visual.
Suggested talk track: “These may both be text files, but they play very different roles. One describes the run. The other is the thing being analyzed or modeled.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
What Good Execution Feels Like

One clear supported way to run an important workflow
Inputs are explicit, not hidden in code
A teammate can follow the docs and succeed
A result can be traced back to code, config, and data
boring to explain
boring to rerun
boring to hand off


Purpose: tie the structure back to the user experience.
Suggested talk track: “Boring is the goal. A healthy run path should feel obvious enough that nobody has to schedule a meeting just to rerun last quarter’s work.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
section: Common Anti-Patterns, and the Fix They Point To
text:
Common Anti-Patterns, and the Fix They Point To







If you see this…
A good next fix is…




sys.path hacks
make imports real and package the code cleanly


hard-coded local paths
move the path into config


script_2024.py, script_2025.py
drive behavior from inputs instead of duplicated files


notebooks as source of truth
try to move reusable logic into src/


main.py just because uv added it
document and support one real entry point




Purpose: connect smells directly to improvements.
Suggested talk track: “These are not signs that someone is bad at their job. They are signs that the project grew without a clear runnable path. Each smell points to a cleaner boundary.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
A “Healthy” Default Repo Shape


repo/
  src/project/
    model.py
    io.py
    validation.py
    app.py
  scripts/
    run_model.py
  configs/
    base.yaml
  notebooks/
    exploratory_analysis.ipynb
  tests/
    test_model.py
  docs/
    running.md


src/ for reusable code
scripts/ for task-focused orchestration
notebooks/ for exploration, not the source of truth
configs/ for declared runs
docs/ for the humans (and some robots)



Purpose: provide a practical default shape rather than a theory.
Suggested talk track: “This is not the one true tree. It is a useful default. The main goal is that a new person can tell what is reusable, what is runnable, and where to look first.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
Tiny Demo Project
The live demo for this talk lives here:
live_demos/2026-03-16_libraries_dont_run_themselves
mini_house/
  src/mini_house/tools.py
  src/mini_house/app.py
  scripts/frame_wall.py
  pyproject.toml

library: a tiny reusable “tool”
script: one part of the work
application: a micro CLI representing the supported interface
same pattern, deliberately stripped down


Purpose: connect the talk to a concrete local example.
Suggested talk track: “I made this deliberately tiny. The goal is not realism. The goal is to show the roles clearly enough that you can point at them in a repo.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
section: Three Improvements You Can Make This Week
text:
Three Improvements You Can Make This Week

Move one chunk of reusable logic out of a notebook or script and into src/
Create one real runnable entry point for an important workflow
Replace one hard-coded path or year with a config value
Make it painfully obvious what someone else should run


Purpose: give the target audience immediate next actions.
Suggested talk track: “You do not need a grand rewrite. Pick one workflow and make the run path clearer this week. That alone makes a project much easier to maintain.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
Bonus: Product Thinking Helps!

Decide who needs to use the thing
Decide how they are supposed to use it
The clearer the user, the clearer the entry point
A repo gets healthier when the supported workflow is designed on purpose
Another developer importing functions needs stable library code
An analyst running a scenario needs one obvious command plus editable config
Is this for another developer to import?
Is this for a teammate to run from the CLI?
What is the smallest successful experience for that user?


Purpose: connect architecture back to user-centered design.
Suggested talk track: “A lot of repo confusion is really a product-definition problem. If we do not decide who the user is and how they should use the workflow, we drift into vague software.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
Closing Thought
Libraries can’t just run themselves. Good repos make that obvious.

Purpose: end on one memorable sentence.
Suggested talk track: “If people leave remembering one thing, I want it to be this: the repo should advertise its runnable path. If that path is obvious, a lot of other architecture decisions get easier.”

title: Libraries Don’t Run Themselves
text:
Discussion

What repo in your world would benefit most from this cleanup?
Which anti-pattern shows up most often?
What would make one of your current projects easier to hand off?


Purpose: invite discussion tied to real work.
Suggested talk track: “I would expect different teams to answer these differently. The useful part is not forcing identical structure everywhere. The useful part is making the runnable path explicit.”

title: GitHub Best Practices
text:
Enable branch protection rules

Protect main (and any other long-lived branches)
Require pull requests before merging
Require minimum 1 approving review
Enforce status checks (CI/CD pipelines, linting, tests)

title: GitHub Best Practices
text:
GitHub Flow

One long-lived branch: main
feature branch → PR → Merge to main
Simple
Best for continuous delivery
Deploy immediately after merge to main

title: GitHub Best Practices
text:
GitFlow

Long-lived branches: main, develop
Short-lived branches: release, hotfix, feature
feature branch -> PR -> Merge to develop
release branch or hotfix -> PR -> Merge to main and develop
Better for large-scale projects, many devs, scheduled releases

title: GitHub Best Practices
text:
🚫 What makes a PR bad?

Large & difficult to review
Many unrelated changes
Poor or no description
Unclear commit history
Fails CI/CD checks
Lacks necessary tests

title: GitHub Best Practices
text:
✅ How to improve PRs

Keep PRs small and focused
Provide a clear summary & context
Write meaningful commit messages
Ensure automated checks pass before requesting review
Add relevant tests & documentation
Explain how a reviewer can test your changes

title: GitHub Best Practices
text:
🚫 Common bad practices

Superficial LGTM approvals
Excessively nitpicky comments
Review lacks a clear status (ie. “Approved”, “Request Changes”, “Close Recommendation”)

title: GitHub Best Practices
text:
✅ How to review effectively

Clearly state your status: “Approved”, “Request Changes”, “Close Recommendation”
Nitpicky or non-blocking comments should be clearly marked
Be constructive and specific
Validate logic, security, performance
Check for coding standards and test coverage
Ensure documentation is updated when necessary

title: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow
text:
What are dotfiles?

Configuration files that live in your $HOME directory
Usually start with a dot (so they’re “hidden”):

.bashrc, .zshrc
.gitconfig
.config/ghostty/config

They control how your tools behave:

shell prompt, aliases, colors
git defaults, editor settings, etc.
crumbs: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow

title: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow
text:
Why care about dotfiles?

New machine? → “Why doesn’t anything feel right?”
Dotfiles in a repo give you:

reproducible setups
easy sharing of configuration (new laptop, new team member)
a place to grow your personal setup over time
crumbs: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow

title: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow
text:
Dotfiles as a developer’s fingerprint

Everyone’s environment is a bit different:

preferred shell, editor, prompt, tools

Your dotfiles encode:

how you navigate
how you debug
how you use git, ssh, etc.

Putting them in a repo:

documents how you work
lets you borrow good ideas from others 😄
crumbs: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow

title: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow
text:
The problem: dotfiles sprawl

Config files live all over $HOME:

~/.bashrc
~/.gitconfig
~/.config/ghostty/config

If you edit them in-place:

hard to sync between machines
easy to accidentally diverge

We’d like a single source of truth in a repo, and a clean way to “deploy” them into $HOME.
crumbs: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow

title: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow
text:
GNU Stow
GNU Stow is a symlink manager.

You keep “real” files in a repo like:

~/dotfiles/
  bash/.bashrc
  bash/.aliases
  git/.gitconfig
  ghostty/.config/ghostty/config

Then you “stow” them into $HOME:

cd ~/dotfiles
stow -t "$HOME" bash git ghostty

stow creates symlinks:

~/.bashrc  -> ~/dotfiles/bash/.bashrc
~/.gitconfig -> ~/dotfiles/git/.gitconfig
crumbs: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow

title: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow
section: Why Stow is nice for dotfiles
text:
Why Stow is nice for dotfiles

Keeps $HOME clean → just symlinks
Easy to enable/disable groups:

stow -t "$HOME" bash
stow -D -t "$HOME" bash   # unstow

Encourages a modular layout:

one folder per “package” (bash, git, ghostty, …)

Works great with git:

version control your dotfiles repo
clone + stow on any machine
crumbs: Taming Your Terminal: Dotfiles & GNU Stow

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Why we’re here
In this session, we’ll walk through the Conventional Commits spec and why it’s useful in practice:

clearer PR history
easier changelogs
(optionally) automated version bumps & releases

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Agenda

What do Conventional Commits solve?
The spec (the minimum you need)
“Breaking changes” + SemVer mapping
Common patterns (scopes, footers, PR merges)
Automated tooling
Resources


If your org uses squash merges, you still benefit: PR title becomes the “commit message.”

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Commit messages drift
Without a shared convention, git history can easily become:

inconsistent (“Fix stuff”, “update”, “WIP”)
hard to scan in git log
painful to turn into release notes
ambiguous for reviewers (“is this a feature or a fix?”)


Emphasize: you’re not “policing,” you’re reducing cognitive load for future-you.

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
What is Conventional Commits?
A lightweight convention for commit messages that are:

human-readable
machine-parseable

It “dovetails with SemVer” by making it easy to identify:

features
fixes
breaking changes


Point to the spec later; for now focus on the shape and why it matters.

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
The core format
<type>[optional scope][optional !]: <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
Examples:

feat: add portfolio export to CSV
fix(api): handle empty scenario set
refactor!: drop legacy endpoint

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Types: pick a small set
Common, widely-used types:

feat — new user-visible behavior
fix — bug fix
docs — documentation only
refactor — code change, no behavior change (ideally)
test — tests only
chore — tooling, build, deps, housekeeping
ci — CI pipeline changes

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Scope
Scopes are optional, but can help answer “where” a change is relevant, especially in larger codebases:
Examples:

feat(ui): add scenario picker
fix(db): prevent deadlock on upsert
docs(api): document auth header

Good scopes are:

small nouns or abbreviations (api, ui, db, auth, deps)
stable over time
not personal (“jackson-fix”)

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Breaking changes
Using a “bang” (!) in the header indicates a breaking change:

feat!: remove v1 scenario fields

If you need to give more context, you can use a footer in the commit description:
feat!: remove v1 scenario fields

BREAKING CHANGE: v1 fields were removed; migrate to v2 schema

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Conventional Commits -> SemVer
The common automated release mapping goes something like:

fix -> PATCH
feat -> MINOR
! or BREAKING CHANGE -> MAJOR

Other types need to be mapped appropriately. This is still just a convention (not magic) and requires engineers to buy into using the convention consistently.

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Example: a “good” conventional commit
fix(auth): refresh tokens on 401

Previously, an expired token caused an infinite retry loop.
Now we refresh once and surface an error if it fails.

Refs: STIT-125, #823

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Migrating from “meh” to “conventional”

Before: ignore stuff
After: chore(git): add build artifacts to .gitignore

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
What about Squash merges?
If the repo you are working on uses squash merges the PR title gets squashed into a commit message. Write the PR title as a conventional commit!

e.g. PR title: feat(ui): add scenario picker

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Automated tooling
Conventional Commits are machine-parseable, so automation can reliably:

Validate messages at commit / PR time
→ catches missing type, malformed scope, or unclear descriptions early
Classify changes from history
→ groups commits into Features, Fixes, Docs, Chores, etc.
Generate release notes / changelogs
→ produces consistent summaries straight from merged work
Decide the next version (if you use SemVer)
→ fix → patch, feat → minor, BREAKING CHANGE / ! → major
Create traceability links
→ connect releases back to PRs/issues via structured footers

title: How to Commit Conventionally
text:
Resources

Conventional Commits spec: https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/
Semantic Versioning (SemVer): https://semver.org/
commitlint (getting started): https://commitlint.js.org/guides/getting-started.html
Release Please: https://github.com/googleapis/release-please
semantic-release: https://github.com/semantic-release/semantic-release
conventional-changelog: https://github.com/conventional-changelog/conventional-changelog

title: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done
text:
Feature Tickets

For features, try to include a user story:

e.g. “As a registered user, I might forget my password and want to regain access to my account securely.”

When possible, supplement the user story with unambiguous acceptance criteria
crumbs: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done

title: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done
text:
Bug Tickets

For bugs, be as detailed as possible!

Often the limiting factor for fixing a bug is reproducing it.

For example:

Environment setup (OS, browser, app version, etc.)
Reproduction Steps (What precise actions lead to the bug?)
Expected behaviour (What should have happened?)
Actual behaviour (What actually happened?)
Screenshots, logs, etc.
crumbs: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done

title: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done
text:
Acceptance Criteria

What must be true for the issue to be considered complete?

Written as bullet points
Example
crumbs: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done

title: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done
text:
Definition of Done:

All acceptance criteria are met
Tests and checks pass
Documentation is updated when relevant
Code is reviewed and merged!
crumbs: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done

title: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done
text:
Setup
brew install gh  # macOS
``

### Authenticating
``
gh auth login
``

### Cloning a Repo
```sh
gh repo clone RMI/web-api-poc
Checking out a PR locally
gh pr checkout 14
crumbs: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done

title: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done
text:
Recap

Write clear, structured tickets
Define acceptance criteria & done criteria
Use meaningful feature branch names
gh CLI simplifies GitHub workflows

Questions?
crumbs: Effective Ticketing & the Definition of Done

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
What is a Unit Test?

A unit test checks that a small unit of code works as expected.
Usually a single function or method.
Helps you catch bugs early, refactor confidently, and build better software.
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
Why Should Data Scientists Care?

Prevent breaking your analysis pipeline accidentally.
Add guardrails for complex logic (e.g., custom transforms).
Make code easier to reuse and maintain.
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
Unit Testing Workflow

Write failing test -> Run with pytest (It should fail)
Write just enough code to pass -> Re-run pytest
See it pass 🎉
Refactor code if needed
Repeat 🔁
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
Best Practices

🧪 Test one case per test, keep them small!
🔁 Test edge cases and common use
🧼 Use clear naming: test_functionname_case
📦 Structure code so logic is in testable functions (not in notebooks!)
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
Code Structure for Testing
Before testing: - Your code should live in a .py file, e.g. my_module.py - Tests should go in a separate file: test_my_module.py
project/
├── my_module.py
├── test_my_module.py
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
Let’s Write a Failing Test
test_my_module.py:
from my_module import multiply_by_two

def test_multiply_by_two():
    assert multiply_by_two(3) == 6
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
Run it with pytest
pytest
Expect output like:
>       assert multiply_by_two(3) == 6
E       NameError: name 'multiply_by_two' is not defined
⬆️ Good! The test is failing as expected.
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
section: Write Just Enough Code to Pass
text:
Write Just Enough Code to Pass
my_module.py:
def multiply_by_two(x):
    return x * 2
Re-run pytest:
collected 1 item
test_my_module.py .                                    [100%]
✅ Green means good!
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
Common Anti-Patterns to Avoid
🚫 Too much in one test - Hard to precisely understand what failed and why
🚫 Testing implementation details - Tests should focus on what your function does and outputs, not how it does it
🚫 Skipping tests for ‘just a script’ - Scripts evolve fast — Make testable functions early and part of your default workflow
🚫 Functions that are hard to test - If a function: - Has side effects - Does too many things - Depends on global state
➡️ It might need to be split into smaller pieces
💡 If it’s hard to write a simple test, your function may need refactoring!
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests
text:
What’s Next?

Try testing:

Edge cases (0, negative numbers, empty lists, incorrect input types)
Your own utility functions

Explore:

Test coverage (pytest-cov)
Mocks and temporary files
crumbs: Python Testing 101: Writing Your First Unit Tests

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
Motivation

Developing and Deploying Apps can be difficult
The 12 Factor App methodology provides guidelines that ensure

clarity,
scalability,
maintainability

Benefits both developers and operations teams.

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
Why are 12 Factor Apps Important?

Consistency and predictability in application behavior.
Simplifies the development lifecycle.
Eases the process of deploying, scaling, and maintaining applications.
Promotes collaboration between development and operations (DevOps).

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
Best Practices
A lot of these concepts are things that are considered best practices, but collected together.

You are probably already do a bunch of these things
You may not know you do it (it just feels right)
You may not know why you do it (it just seems to work)
You may do it because you were told to (it isn’t broken)

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
Benefits for Developers

Clear guidelines for application structure and behavior.
Simplified onboarding and reduced cognitive load.
Easier debugging and local development.
Predictable dependencies and environments.

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
Benefits for DevOps/Operations

Streamlined deployment and automated management.
Improved reliability and faster issue resolution.
Easier monitoring and logging.

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
The 12 Factors (Brief Overview)

Codebase: One codebase tracked in version control, many deploys.
Dependencies: Explicitly declare and isolate dependencies.
Config: Store configuration in the environment.
Backing Services: Treat backing services as attached resources.
Build, Release, Run: Strictly separate build and run stages.
Processes: Execute the app as one or more stateless processes.

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
The 12 Factors (Brief Overview)

Port Binding: Export services via port binding.
Concurrency: Scale out via the process model.
Disposability: Maximize robustness with fast startup and graceful shutdown.
Dev/Prod Parity: Keep development, staging, and production similar.
Logs: Treat logs as event streams.
Admin Processes: Run admin tasks as one-off processes.

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
Examples of Common Pitfalls

Configuration hardcoded in source code.
Dependencies not clearly defined or versioned.
Stateful processes causing unpredictable behavior.
Significant differences between development and production environments.

title: 12 Factor Applications (Pt 1)
text:
Getting Started with 12 Factor Apps

Review your current projects against the 12 factors.
Incrementally adopt factors as you enhance or refactor projects.
Foster collaboration and awareness across development and operations teams.

title: OKRs 101
section: 🏎️ Will it make the car go faster?
text:
🏎️ Will it make the car go faster?

Red Bull’s Formula 1 Racing team asks one question before every decision:

“Will it make the car go faster?”
If the answer is no, it’s not a priority.

Everyone from the pit crew to the strategist knows the same goal.
For RMI, that question might be more like:

“Will it drive decarbonization?”
This question helps us focus our energy and creativity on work that accelerates impact — the equivalent of making our car go faster.

title: OKRs 101
section: 🎯 Why are we talking about OKRs?
text:
🎯 Why are we talking about OKRs?
Objectives and Key Results help bridge our day-to-day work with SPD’s broader mission:

Enabling public and private sector actors to make energy transition decisions using SPD products.
They:

Define what to prioritize and (often more crucially) what not to.

Help every team member align on outcomes that move the needle.

Connect organization, team, and personal objectives to measurable outcomes.

title: OKRs 101
text:
⚙️ OKR Fundamentals
Good OKRs always focus on outcomes and not activities.

Objective: What we want to achieve
Key Results: How we’ll measure success
Each KR must be measurable and collectively signal achievement of the Objective.

title: OKRs 101
text:
🧩 Anatomy of a Good OKR

Example Objective:

Ensure every SPD product delivers impact and engagement, not just technical completion.

Key Results:

All new project concepts validated with real users (0–1.0)

All new project proposals budgeted for comms & design (0–1.0)

All product launches include a “splash” marketing moment (0–1.0)

Collaboration process defined with Influence team (0–1.0)

title: OKRs 101
text:
🧮 Stretch and Grade
If you always hit 100%, your goals aren’t ambitious enough.

Key Results shouldn’t be binary “done/not done.”

We use a 0–1.0 scale to track progress.

~0.7 indicates we’re at the right level of stretch — ambitious but achievable.

OKRs are designed to push learning, not punish shortfalls.

title: OKRs 101
text:
⚠️ Common Pitfalls

OKRs should be:

Few in number

Measurable

Focused on value, not activity

Avoid:

Confusing outputs: 🚫 “deploy web application to production and publish blog post”)
with outcomes: ✅ “X% of pilot users log in more than twice within the first month”)
Setting too many OKRs — focus is power

Writing vague or unmeasurable goals (“do more research”)

title: OKRs 101
text:
🏗️ How SPD Uses OKRs

We decide what’s most important each quarter

We measure progress toward outcomes, not outputs

We keep workload realistic to prevent burnout

We maintain alignment and velocity across the team

title: OKRs 101
section: 🧭 How Are OKRs Different From OOMs?
text:
🧭 How Are OKRs Different From OOMs?







Concept
Purpose




OOMs
Report organizational milestones & long-term yearly targets


OKRs
Ground each quarter in actionable, measurable goals that connect to daily work and sprint backlogs

title: OKRs 101
text:
💻 Hands-On — OKRs in Jira Goals

Demo time!

title: OKRs 101
text:
📚 Resources

Google OKR Guidance
Google OKR Video
